Capstone Project - Walmart Dataset¶

# Importing basic Libraries

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import math

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
def fxn():
    warnings.warn("deprecated", DeprecationWarning)
with warnings.catch_warnings(action="ignore"):
    fxn()

filepath = r'D:\Harpreet\Documents\Learning\Intellipaat- DS & AI 2025\ML\2025.11.16- Wallmart Project\Walmart_Dataset.csv'
walmart_df = pd.read_csv(filepath)
walmart_df

6432 45 12-10-2012 734464.36 0 54.47 4.000 192.327265 8.667
6433 45 19-10-2012 718125.53 0 56.47 3.969 192.330854 8.667
6434 45 26-10-2012 760281.43 0 58.85 3.882 192.308899 8.667

6435 rows × 8 columns

Observations

  • We have 45 stores. So to see performance of each store, we may need to take input of store number.
  • It seems there is Temperature data also, indicating climatic conditions may be affecting the sales in some way.
  • Weekly sales are given, there is a consistent span of 7 days of records. Weekly sales must be the amount of sales happened in that week span.
  • CPI is the consumer price index, which indicates a ratio of consumption; sort of inflation factor. Probably indicating high CPI is high prices of Fuel.
  • and parallelly, Uneployment rate may be a trend of records of the municipal.
  • Probably the holiday flag may be that there might have been a holiday or more in the specific week
In [43]:
# Firstly we get a date index to begin with

walmart_df['Date']= pd.to_datetime(walmart_df['Date'], errors='coerce', format="%d-%m-%Y")  # converts the Date object to datetime format
                                                                                            # included the format parameter to avoid errors moving forward
walmart_df.set_index("Date", inplace=True)     # make the Date column as the index
# walmart_df.sort_index(inplace=True, ascending=True)    # sort as per ascending order

walmart_df.info()

2010-02-19 1 1611968.17 0 39.93 2.514 211.289143 8.106
2010-02-26 1 1409727.59 0 46.63 2.561 211.319643 8.106
2010-03-05 1 1554806.68 0 46.50 2.625 211.350143 8.106

1. You are provided with the weekly sales data for their various outlets. Use statistical analysis, EDA, outlier analysis, and handle the missing values to come up with various insights that can give them a clear perspective on the following:

  • a. If the weekly sales are affected by the unemployment rate, if yes - which stores are suffering the most?
  • b. If the weekly sales show a seasonal trend, when and what could be the reason?
  • c. Does temperature affect the weekly sales in any manner?
  • d. How is the Consumer Price index affecting the weekly sales of various stores?
  • e. Top performing stores according to the historical data.
  • f. The worst performing store, and how significant is the difference between the highest and lowest performing stores.

2. Use predictive modeling techniques to forecast the sales for each store for the next 12 weeks.

Here we will solve the Q1 of the given project.¶

In [45]:
# Checking for duplicates or Null values

print(f"Duplicate items = {walmart_df.duplicated().sum()}")
print("---------------------")
print(f"Null Items : \n{walmart_df.isnull().sum()}")

Duplicate items = 0
---------------------

Weekly_Sales    0
Holiday_Flag    0
Temperature     0
Fuel_Price      0
CPI             0
Unemployment    0
dtype: int64

There aren't any duplicates or Nulls. We can proceed with the EDA

In [46]:
number_of_stores= int(walmart_df['Store'].nunique())
stores= walmart_df['Store'].unique()
print(f'Dataset contains complete data from {number_of_stores} stores.')

Dataset contains complete data from 45 stores.
In [47]:
plt.figure(figsize=(13,4.5))
store_sales = walmart_df.groupby('Store', as_index=False)['Weekly_Sales'].sum().sort_values(by='Weekly_Sales', ascending=False)

sns.barplot(
    data=store_sales,
    x='Store',
    y='Weekly_Sales',
    palette='viridis'
)

plt.title('Total Sales by Store', fontsize=16, color='gold', fontweight='semibold')
plt.xlabel('Store', fontsize=12, color='white')
plt.ylabel('Total Weekly Sales', fontsize=12, color='white')
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

1/a ... If the weekly sales are affected by the unemployment rate, if yes - which stores are suffering the most?¶
In [50]:
df= pd.DataFrame(walmart_df[['Store', 'Weekly_Sales', 'Unemployment']])
corr_matrix= df.corr()
corr_matrix
Out[50]:
Store Weekly_Sales Unemployment
Store 1.000000 -0.335332 0.223531
Weekly_Sales -0.335332 1.000000 -0.106176
Unemployment 0.223531 -0.106176 1.000000
In [51]:
# Generate and save a heatmap for each store

col_plots= 5
rows_plots= math.ceil(number_of_stores / col_plots)
fig, axes= plt.subplots(rows_plots, col_plots, figsize=(20,20))
axes= axes.flatten()

correlations= []

for i, store_no in enumerate(stores):
    store_data = df[df['Store'] == store_no][['Weekly_Sales', 'Unemployment']]
    store_data.rename(columns={'Weekly_Sales':'W', 'Unemployment':'U'}, inplace=True)
    corr_matrix = store_data.corr()

    corr_value= store_data['W'].corr(store_data['U'])
    correlations.append((store_no, corr_value))

    sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', ax=axes[i], cbar=False, vmin=-1, vmax=1, square=True)
    axes[i].set_title(f'Store {store_no}')

for j in range (i+1, len(axes)):
    fig.delaxes(axes[j])

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [52]:
# We will save the correlations in each store and sort it for further observations.
correlations_sorted= sorted(correlations, key=lambda x: x[1])

for store_id, corr in correlations_sorted:
    print(f"Store {store_id}: correlation = {corr:.4f}")

In [53]:
# plotting a bar geraph of the correlations Values

corr_df= pd.DataFrame(correlations_sorted, columns=['Store_ID', 'Corr_Value'])  
# Plotting
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
bars = plt.bar(corr_df['Store_ID'], corr_df['Corr_Value'], color='skyblue')
plt.xlabel("Store ID")
plt.xticks(corr_df['Store_ID'], rotation=90)
plt.ylabel("Correlation (W vs U)")
plt.title("Correlation between Weekly Sales (W) and Unemployment (U) per Store (Sorted)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The plot tells us that the impact of unemployment on weekly sales is store-specific. Some stores are resilient or even benefit when unemployment rises, while others are vulnerable.

The most affected store is Store No. 36 with corr matrix value at 0.83.

1/b ... If the weekly sales show a seasonal trend, when and what could be the reason?¶

Store Weekly_Sales Holiday_Flag Temperature Fuel_Price CPI Unemployment
Date
2010-02-05 1 1643690.90 0 42.31 2.572 211.096358 8.106
2010-02-12 1 1641957.44 1 38.51 2.548 211.242170 8.106
2010-02-19 1 1611968.17 0 39.93 2.514 211.289143 8.106

In [55]:
df= pd.DataFrame(walmart_df, columns=['Store', "Weekly_Sales"])

fig, axes = plt.subplots(15, 3, figsize=(30,60), sharex=True, sharey=True)
axes = axes.flatten()

# Plot each store's data
for i, store in enumerate(sorted(df['Store'].unique())):
    ax = axes[i]
    store_data = df[df['Store'] == store]
    ax.plot(store_data.index, store_data['Weekly_Sales'], color='steelblue')
    ax.set_title(f'Store {store}', fontsize=10)
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=45, labelsize=12)
    ax.tick_params(axis='y', labelsize=12)
    if i % 15 == 0:
        ax.set_ylabel('Weekly Sales', fontsize=20)

# Hide any unused subplots
for j in range(len(stores), len(axes)):
    fig.delaxes(axes[j])

# Add main title and layout adjustments
fig.suptitle('Weekly Sales vs Date for 45 Stores', fontsize=18)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.95])

plt.show()

A Seasonal Trend is observed almost around all the stores. There is a peak around Novem-December every year, perhaps becsuase of Christmas and Holidays. Some stores show very high peak of sales records, while some not as much. In some stores, there doesn't seem to be seasonality, perhaps because of cultural differences, or may be some other reasons not clear from the given dataset.

corr_matrix
Out[57]:

In [58]:
# Generate and save a heatmap for each store

col_plots= 5
rows_plots= math.ceil(number_of_stores / col_plots)
fig, axes= plt.subplots(rows_plots, col_plots, figsize=(20,20))
axes= axes.flatten()

correlations= []

for i, store_no in enumerate(stores):
    store_data = df[df['Store'] == store_no][['Weekly_Sales', 'Temperature']]
    store_data.rename(columns={'Weekly_Sales':'W', 'Temperature':'T'}, inplace=True)
    corr_matrix = store_data.corr()

    corr_value= store_data['W'].corr(store_data['T'])
    correlations.append((store_no, corr_value))

    sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', ax=axes[i], cbar=False, vmin=-1, vmax=1, square=True)
    axes[i].set_title(f'Store {store_no}')

for j in range (i+1, len(axes)):
    fig.delaxes(axes[j])

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [59]:
corr_df= pd.DataFrame(correlations, columns=['Store_ID', 'Corr_Value'])  
# Plotting
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
bars = plt.bar(corr_df['Store_ID'], corr_df['Corr_Value'], color='skyblue')
plt.xlabel("Store ID")
plt.xticks(corr_df['Store_ID'], rotation=90)
plt.ylabel("Correlation (W vs T)")
plt.title("Correlation between Weekly Sales (W) and Temperature (T) per Store (Sorted)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

We can clearly see the trend that temperatures affect the weekly Sales given that when the temperatures are lower, the sales are higher in most of the stores. In some stores the trend is reverse, probably affected by other factors which are not clear from the given data set.

1/d ... How is the Consumer Price index affecting the weekly sales of various stores?¶
In [60]:
# Creating bar graph of correlation between Weekly Sales and Unemployment per store
df= pd.DataFrame(walmart_df[['Store', 'Weekly_Sales', 'CPI']])

# Compute correlation per store
correlations = df.groupby("Store")[["Weekly_Sales", "CPI"]].corr().iloc[0::2,-1].reset_index()
correlations = correlations[["Store", "CPI"]].rename(columns={"CPI": "Corr_Value"})

# Plotting
plt.figure(figsize=(13, 6))
bars = plt.bar(correlations["Store"].astype(str), correlations["Corr_Value"], color='skyblue')
plt.xlabel("Store Number")
plt.ylabel("Correlation (Weekly Sales vs CPI)")
plt.title("Correlation between Weekly Sales and CPI per Store")
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)

# Annotate bars
for bar in bars:
    yval = bar.get_height()
    plt.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, yval, f"{yval:.2f}", ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
1/e ... Top performing stores according to the historical data.¶
In [61]:
# Best of Average Weekly Sales is performed by this store:
Avg_WeeklySales_StoreWise= pd.DataFrame(walmart_df.groupby('Store')['Weekly_Sales'].mean()).reset_index()
Avg_WeeklySales_StoreWise.sort_values(by='Weekly_Sales', ascending=True, inplace=True)

# Create bar plot
plt.figure(figsize=(13, 6))
plt.bar(Avg_WeeklySales_StoreWise['Store'].astype(str), Avg_WeeklySales_StoreWise['Weekly_Sales'], color='skyblue')
plt.xlabel('Store Number', fontsize=10)
plt.ylabel('Average Weekly Sales', fontsize=10)
plt.title('Average Weekly Sales per Store', fontsize=14)
plt.xticks(Avg_WeeklySales_StoreWise['Store'])
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
